retrieved_docs.content: In neurosurgery, the posterior auricular artery is grafted in rare instances to restore the blood supply in the circle of Willis. The restoration of blood supply in the circle of Willis is via bypass grafting. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Auriculotemporal Nerve -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The auriculotemporal nerve contains secretomotor, somatosensory, and vasomotor fibers. This nerve originates from the sensory nucleus of the trigeminal nerve in the pons. The mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve exits the cranium out of the foramen ovale. After exiting the skull, the auriculotemporal nerve branches off where it has a superior and posterior root encircling the middle meningeal artery before joining together to form one nerve. After both roots fuse, it continues posterior to the lateral pterygoid muscle and travels between the sphenomandibular ligament and the neck of the mandible. The nerve lies posterior the parotid gland where it gives off parasympathetic and vasomotor fibers before it travels just inferior to the superior aspect of the mandibular condyle of the temporal mandibular joint.  As it continues to travel superior, past the temporal mandibular joint, in the infratemporal fossa, it shares an intimate relationship with the superficial temporal artery. Along its path, it anastomoses with the facial nerve on the and the otic ganglion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Posterior Auricular Artery -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior auricular artery can also be used as a landmark during dissection to find the facial nerve. The facial nerve exits the stylomastoid foramen. Perfusion of this foramen is via the posterior auricular artery. [2] [3] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Auriculotemporal Nerve -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The auriculotemporal nerve is a tributary of the mandibular division of cranial nerve five, the trigeminal nerve. It contains sensory, vasomotor, and parasympathetic fibers. It has a superior and inferior root that originates from the trigeminal nerve, encircles the middle meningeal artery ultimately traveling inferior to the temporomandibular joint and up the temple providing sensation to parts of the ear, temporomandibular joint, scalp, as well as secretory and vasomotor innervation to the parotid gland. This nerve is clinically relevant in the setting of temporal mandibular joint disease repair, postoperative complications in a parotidectomy, and trigeminal neuralgia.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Posterior Auricular Artery -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior auricular artery perfuses the majority of the ear and the scalp posterior to the ear. This perfusion region will also supply blood to the greater auricular nerve (C2, C3 spinal roots) and the lesser occipital nerve (C2 spinal roots). The facial nerve also receives perfusion from the posterior auricular artery at the stylomastoid foramen opening.

retrieved_docs.content: The superficial temporal branches run posterior to the superficial temporal artery. These branches provide somatosensory innervation to the skin over the temple. This branch also anastomoses with the facial nerve and zygomaticotemporal nerve, a branch of the maxillary division of cranial nerve five, the trigeminal nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Scalp Veins -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Several nerves are relevant and proximal to the scalp veins, with varying relationships anatomically to these structures. The supratrochlear nerve is located medially in the supratrochlear vein, whereas the medial branch of the supraorbital nerve is just deep in the supratrochlear vein. The lateral branch of the supraorbital nerve is just medial and deep to the supraorbital vein. The temporal branches of the facial nerve are located between the zygomatic orbital vein (a non-scalp vein) inferiorly and the frontal branch of the superficial temporal vein superiorly and posteriorly. The auriculotemporal nerve runs anteriorly and deep to the superficial temporal artery and vein. The posterior auricular artery and vein are sandwiched between the posterior auricular nerve anteriorly and the great auricular nerve just deep and posteroinferiorly. The occipital artery and vein are the lesser and greater occipital nerve branches, superficially anteriorly and posteriorly. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
retrieved_docs.content: embolie; arteria basilaris, referred to in Arabic as انصمام الشريان المخي, denotes an embolic occlusion of the basilar artery. This condition occurs when a thrombus or other embolic material blocks the basilar artery, which supplies blood to the brainstem, cerebellum, and posterior cerebral circulation. The resulting ischemia can lead to severe neurological deficits, including loss of consciousness, cranial nerve palsies, motor weakness, and potentially fatal outcomes if not promptly recognized and treated. Early diagnosis through neuroimaging and rapid reperfusion strategies are critical for improving patient prognosis and minimizing long‑term disability.

The Structure of intracranial artery has a finding site of تصلب عصيدي في المخ, signifying the presence of intracranial atherosclerosis, and it is an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck. This relationship suggests that endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels can affect intracranial arterial structures, linking vascular surgical interventions to intracranial vascular pathology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how a hierarchical set of arterial therapeutic interventions, catheterization techniques, and vascular findings are interconnected within the broader framework of vascular medicine, providing clinicians with a comprehensive view of procedural options and their anatomical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; it is classified as a vessel of the head and neck. Arteria basilaris itself is a documented site of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries. The basilar artery is also the anatomic location where transient cerebral ischemia (نوبة إقفارية عابرة) occurs, representing a transient ischemic attack. Occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries encompasses basilar‑artery narrowing (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي) and basilar‑artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي), both linked to cerebral atherosclerosis and to the development of احتشاء دماغي. These vascular abnormalities are classified within central nervous system extracranial vascular diseases (أمراض وعائية إقْفارِيّة في الجهاز العصبي المركزي) and constitute a specific form of arterial occlusion (انسداد الشرايين). Embolism of the basilar artery (embolie; arteria basilaris) maps to the broader category of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries and is a subtype of cerebral artery

retrieved_docs.content: occlusion or stenosis—whether symptomatic, asymptomatic, thrombotic, embolic, or associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis—is the central pathogenic substrate that leads to cerebral (and occasionally cardiac) infarction, informs diagnostic imaging, and guides interventions such as endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2670
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of basilar artery, a condition in which the basilar artery is blocked without producing noticeable symptoms, is evaluated in the context of a patient’s general wellbeing, with clinicians interpreting the significance of this silent vascular finding as part of an overall assessment of health status.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: arterial blockage. Surgical intervention, such as endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck (استئصال باطنة الشريان), is indicated for arterial thrombosis/embolism and is performed at the arteria basilaris region. The concept “Occlusie en stenose van precerebrale arteriën, niet resulterend in cerebraal infarct” is recognized as a parent condition, emphasizing that not all precerebral arterial narrowing leads to infarction. Overall, the knowledge base connects infarction morphology, vascular occlusion, precerebral arterial disease, transient ischemic events, atherosclerotic pathology, and endovascular or surgical treatment within a unified framework of cerebrovascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: situating basilar‑artery embolism as a type of cerebral‑artery embolism (انصمام الشريان المخي) within the broader cerebrovascular disease framework. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how occlusive disease of precerebral and coronary arteries produces infarction in the brain and heart, how the basilar artery serves as a central anatomical locus, and how imaging findings, morphological patterns, and surgical interventions are integrated into patient assessment and management.

Structural evaluation of intracranial arteries (Structure of intracranial artery) identifies تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis) as a finding site, which is itself derived from precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis, highlighting the pathophysiological link between arterial wall disease and luminal narrowing. These intracranial vessels may serve as indirect targets for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure classified under استئصال باطنة الشريان (endarterectomy). استئصال باطنة الشريان is listed as a member of حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ), reflecting its therapeutic role in managing thrombotic and embolic arterial conditions. Overall, the network emphasizes that precerebral arterial occlusion or stenosis—whether symptomatic, asymptomatic, thrombotic, embolic, or associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis—is the central pathogenic substrate that leads to cerebral (and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: artery.” This structural involvement provides an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (“endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck”), which falls under the broader surgical category of endarterectomy (“استئصال باطنة الشريان”) and addresses both thrombosis and arterial embolism. Therapeutically, heparin is noted as an anticoagulant that may be employed to treat brain infarction resulting from embolic or thrombotic mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate clinical presentations (syncope, asymptomatic occlusion), imaging findings (intracranial atherosclerosis), procedural interventions (endarterectomy), pharmacologic therapy (heparin), and hierarchical disease classification, providing a comprehensive view of how precerebral arterial occlusion or stenosis can manifest as various forms of cerebral infarction—sometimes silently—and how it fits within the broader neuro‑vascular disease framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction caused by embolism of the precerebral arteries, by thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, or by occlusion of the basilar artery is described with the morphological hallmark الاحتشاء (infarction), a finding that is also linked to the cardiac entity احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ تَحْتَ الشِّغاف (heart‑muscle infarction). الاحتشاء is in turn related to انسداد الأوعية الدموية (vascular occlusion), emphasizing that tissue infarction typically follows an occlusive vascular event. Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion is mapped to occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, which is identified as the upstream source of both embolic and thrombotic precerebral arterial infarcts and as the direct cause of basilar‑artery infarction; thus, occlusion/stenosis of precerebral arteries serves as a central mechanistic node linking these stroke entities. Within the broader nosology, the category Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System is a child of transient cerebral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery includes the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component. This intracranial arterial structure is a recognized site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis). Although direct surgical access to intracranial arteries is limited, endarterectomy of vessels of the head and neck is considered an indirect procedural approach that may affect the structure of intracranial artery. Understanding the relationships among the basilar artery, intracranial atherosclerotic disease, and head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is important for comprehensive cerebrovascular assessment and treatment planning.
